doc.title: Operating activities — direct method
doc.event: Lund Co.: sales $500,000 (accounts receivable rose $15,000); cost of goods sold $300,000 (inventory fell $8,000, accounts payable rose $12,000); operating expenses excluding depreciation $25,000 (prepaid expenses rose $2,000). Compute each operating cash flow directly.

variants.label: Convert each income-statement line to its cash twin

computationPaths.narration: The direct method converts line by line: each accrual number ± the change in its related balance-sheet account = the cash version. Same 185,000 destination as the indirect method — different road.

steps.label: Depreciation
steps.formulaText: IGNORED — no cash line exists for it

memorize.body: Direct conversions: customers = sales − ΔAR↑ · suppliers = COGS + ΔInv↑ − ΔAP↑ (two steps: to purchases, then to cash) · expenses = accrual amount + ΔPrepaid↑ − ΔAccrued liab↑. Depreciation gets no line at all.
Suppliers is the two-hop conversion everyone fumbles: COGS → purchases (inventory change) → cash paid (payables change). One hop at a time.

questions.prompt: Sales 500,000; accounts receivable increased 15,000. Cash received from customers =

distractors.feedback: A RISING receivable means some sales weren't collected yet — cash runs BELOW sales: 500,000 − 15,000 = 485,000.
500,000 is accrual-basis sales. The direct method wants the cash twin, and the 15,000 AR increase is still out there uncollected.
